# RFC-0001: Large-Model SGLang Server-Arg Search Methodology

> **Version:** 0.3.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0001:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0001c-summary"></a>

This RFC specifies a methodology for searching the server-argument space of large
SGLang inference deployments to find configurations that best trade off throughput
against latency under a defined service-level objective (SLO).

**Scope:** The search procedure, its loop structure, the source of truth for
configurations, the optimization objective, acceptance criteria, and provenance
requirements. The methodology is model-agnostic; Nemotron-3-Ultra-550B (NVFP4/FP8)
is its first application. Speculative decoding (EAGLE / spec-decode) is explicitly
out of scope for this methodology (see [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-scope)).

**Out of scope:** The specific known-best argument values for any given model, the
results-presentation tooling beyond the requirement that a config's provenance be
recorded, and the choice of orchestration implementation.

**Rationale:** A naive full-factorial grid over server arguments × workload axes ×
precision is infeasible for 550B-class models, where every restart-required
configuration costs a full server relaunch, weight load, and warmup before a single
measurement. This RFC defines obligations that keep the search tractable, the results
comparable across runs, and the chosen configurations trustworthy.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE] Search scope and exclusions (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-scope"></a>

The methodology MUST classify every tunable server argument into exactly one of three
buckets before a search begins: *fixed* (a known-best value that is never varied),
*candidate* (varied during the search), or *constrained* (governed by an explicit
illegal-combination rule). The candidate set SHOULD contain at most ten arguments.

Speculative decoding (EAGLE / spec-decode and equivalent draft-model techniques) MUST
NOT be treated as a candidate argument under this methodology; it is out of scope. A
search that wishes to evaluate speculative decoding MUST do so under a separate effort,
because it alters the argument taxonomy and the comparability baseline.

**Rationale:** Bounding and bucketing the argument space up front is what makes the
search tractable; an unbounded candidate set reproduces the infeasible full-factorial
grid. Speculative decoding changes both the arg taxonomy and the meaning of a
throughput number, so folding it into the same sweep would break comparability with the
non-speculative baseline.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-LOOP-STRUCTURE] Outer/inner loop separation (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-loop-structure"></a>

The search MUST be organized as two nested loops. The outer loop MUST iterate over
restart-required configuration (for example tensor/expert parallelism, attention
backend, quantization, static memory fraction, CUDA-graph capture settings, and
chunked-prefill size); each outer iteration MUST relaunch the server. The inner loop
MUST iterate over workload axes (batch size, input/output sequence lengths, and
concurrency) against a single live server without relaunching it.

Workload axes MUST NOT be expanded into the outer loop as if they were restart-required
configuration.

**Rationale:** Restart-required configuration costs a full relaunch, weight load, and
warmup per value, whereas workload axes are client-side and free to sweep against one
running server. Conflating the two multiplies expensive restarts by axes that need
none, which is the dominant cost error in a naive grid.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-PRECISION-BRANCH] Precision as a branch, not a grid axis (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-precision-branch"></a>

Numeric precision (for example NVFP4 versus FP8) MUST be treated as a top-level branch
of the search, not as a grid axis multiplied into every configuration. Each precision
branch MAY have its own checkpoint, fixed-argument set, and baseline. A per-branch
baseline is scoped within its branch and MUST NOT replace the cross-run comparability
anchor required by [RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor).

**Rationale:** Distinct precisions typically correspond to different checkpoints and
fundamentally different deployments; treating precision as an inner grid axis multiplies
the entire search by a factor while comparing configurations that are not
substitutable.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-CONFIG-SOURCE] Versioned config as source of truth (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-config-source"></a>

The single source of truth for which configurations are searched MUST be a
version-controlled configuration file in the repository. A configuration generator MUST
emit only valid permutations, applying the constraint rules from [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-scope) to
prune illegal combinations before any server launch.

External spreadsheet or database tools (for example Lark) MUST NOT be used as the
configuration generator. Such tools MAY be used only as a downstream results viewer.

**Rationale:** A versioned config file gives validation, incompatibility rules, and
provenance; a spreadsheet used as a generator produces invalid combinations that are
discovered only by wasting expensive launches.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY] Sensitivity-then-focused-grid search (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-search-strategy"></a>

The search MUST NOT enumerate a full-factorial grid over the candidate arguments.
Instead it MUST proceed in stages: first a one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) sensitivity
sweep that varies a single candidate argument around a known-good baseline, then a
focused joint grid restricted to only those arguments that BOTH showed sensitivity in
the OFAT stage AND plausibly interact. Arguments that did not survive the OFAT stage
MUST be pinned to their OFAT-best value during the focused grid. The set of arguments
admitted to the focused grid, and the rationale for judging them to interact, MUST be
recorded so the selection is inspectable.

**Rationale:** Serving arguments are mostly independent, so OFAT eliminates the large
majority of the grid while revealing which arguments matter and in which direction; a
joint grid is reserved for the few arguments where interaction is plausible. Recording
the admitted set turns the otherwise-subjective interaction judgment into an inspectable
artifact.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE] Pareto frontier under an SLO (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-objective"></a>

A service-level objective (SLO) MUST be defined before the search begins. The SLO's
gating bound MUST be a decode latency bound: a per-token (inter-token) latency limit,
equivalently a minimum steady-state decode throughput. A time-to-first-token (TTFT)
target MAY also be defined, but TTFT MUST be treated as a reported, non-gating metric — a
configuration MUST NOT be excluded from the results on the basis of TTFT alone.

Decode performance is the first-class objective. The throughput axis MUST be the
steady-state decode throughput at the operating context length: the decode rate measured
over a window of consecutive decode steps at the run's longest sequence position,
excluding the first decode step after prefill. It MUST NOT be a throughput averaged over
an output sequence that mixes in inexpensive early-context tokens. The window size MUST be
fixed before the search and recorded; every decode-throughput measurement MUST be tagged
with the context length at which it was taken, so that measurements are compared only at
equivalent sequence positions.

The optimization objective MUST be the decode-throughput-versus-decode-latency Pareto
frontier under the decode SLO. Configurations MUST NOT be ranked by raw throughput alone.

**Rationale:** For large-context serving, decode is memory-bandwidth-bound on the KV cache
and its cost is governed by the current sequence length, not by how that length was
reached; per-token decode latency is what a user experiences continuously, whereas TTFT is
paid once. A decode throughput averaged over a short output is dominated by warm-up
transients, and over a long output is diluted by cheap early-context tokens, so the
steady-state rate over a window at a stated context length is the only comparable signal.
Gating on TTFT would discard configurations that sustain excellent decode merely because
prefilling a long prompt is slow."""

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE] Accuracy acceptance gate (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-quality-gate"></a>

A configuration MUST pass an accuracy acceptance gate before it is eligible to appear in
the acceptable results. The accuracy gate's pass/fail threshold and evaluation dataset
MUST be defined before the search begins. The accuracy gate MUST be evaluated for each
precision branch (see [RFC-0001:C-PRECISION-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-precision-branch)). For this clause, the *acceptable
results* are the ranked decode-throughput-versus-decode-latency Pareto frontier defined by
[RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-objective); the *measurement record stream* is the full set of recorded
measurements defined by [RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement).

A configuration that fails the accuracy gate MUST be excluded from eligibility at every
stage of the search, regardless of its throughput or latency. A value whose OFAT
configuration failed the gate MUST NOT be used to pin a non-gridded argument
([RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-search-strategy)). A failing configuration MUST NOT be carried forward as an
eligible point in the focused grid. A failing configuration MUST NOT appear in the
acceptable results. If a precision branch has no gate-passing value with which to pin a
required non-gridded argument, or if no configuration in the branch passes the gate, the
search MUST report that branch as having no acceptable configuration rather than emitting a
degraded one.

A failing configuration's measurement MUST still be recorded in the same measurement record
stream that carries passing configurations, extended with its accuracy-gate result and a
pass/fail flag ([RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement)), so that a speed-versus-quality trade-off
remains inspectable over that stream. A failing configuration MUST NOT thereby be promoted
into the acceptable results; it is retained only for inspection and possible explicit human
override. An override that admits a flagged configuration MUST itself be recorded in the
measurement record stream.

**Rationale:** A throughput- and latency-only search will otherwise select fast but
quality-degraded configurations, which is especially likely across precision branches such
as NVFP4 versus FP8. Fixing the threshold up front (as with the SLO in
[RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-objective)) prevents the bar from being moved to fit the results. Excluding
failures at every search stage — not only the final ranking — stops a quality-degraded value
from steering OFAT pinning or grid admission. Recording and flagging the failure in the
measurement record stream rather than discarding it preserves the measured trade-off for a
human to judge, while keeping the acceptable results free of degraded configurations.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT] Warmup, repeats, and provenance (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-measurement"></a>

Each measured configuration MUST be warmed up before measurement and MUST be measured
with at least two repeat runs. Each recorded result MUST capture the full reproducibility
provenance: the configuration identity (for example a content hash of the config), the
workload point, the measured metrics, and the execution environment (at minimum the
SGLang commit, the kernel/library versions, and all cluster networking environment
variables in effect).

**Rationale:** Warmup dominates first-measurement timing on 550B-class models, and a
single unrepeated measurement is noise; without recorded environment provenance, numbers
from different runs cannot be compared or reproduced.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR] Comparability anchor (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor"></a>

The search MUST retain a stable baseline benchmark whose methodology does not change
across runs, to anchor comparability with prior results. New benchmarking tooling MAY be
introduced for richer scenario and latency-component metrics, but before it is relied
upon its results MUST be reconciled against the stable baseline benchmark on an
equivalent workload (for example confirming time-to-first-token agreement against an
already-running server).

**Rationale:** Historical baselines were produced with a specific built-in benchmark;
silently switching tools makes new numbers incomparable to the prior record. Reconciling
a new tool against the anchor before adoption prevents undetected baseline drift.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-WORKLOAD-STAGING] Workload cost-role staging (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-workload-staging"></a>

The search MUST separate workloads by cost role. Short-output workloads (a long prompt with
a small number of decode tokens, for example a 60k-token input with tens of output tokens)
MUST serve as the fast-iteration signal that drives the argument search: every decode step
of such a workload sits at approximately the same long context length, so the steady-state
decode throughput at that length (RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE) is obtained directly from its tail
decode window. Long-output workloads (a large number of decode tokens) MUST NOT be run
across the full candidate set, and MUST NOT be run during the OFAT or focused-grid stages
of RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY; they MUST be reserved for characterizing the final chosen
configuration(s) in the reported result.

A decode-throughput number obtained from a short-output fast-iteration workload at context
length L, and one obtained from a long-output workload as its decode trajectory passes
through the same context length L, are measurements of the same decode regime. When both
are available they SHOULD be reconciled within the tolerance of RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR;
a disagreement beyond that tolerance MUST be treated as an unreconciled measurement.

**Rationale:** Running a long-output workload across the full candidate set multiplies the
most expensive part of the search — sustained decode — across every configuration, which is
the cost error this methodology exists to avoid. Because decode throughput depends only on
the current context length, a cheap short-output run at a long prompt length yields the same
steady-state decode signal needed to rank arguments, leaving the expensive long-output
characterization for the few configurations that survive the search."""

*Since: v0.2.0*

---

## Changelog

### v0.3.0 (2026-06-09)

Tighten C-QUALITY-GATE: accuracy gate filters eligibility at every search stage (OFAT pinning, focused-grid admission, final results); failing configs recorded and flagged in the measurement stream for trade-off inspection, never promoted into the acceptable results

### v0.2.0 (2026-06-09)

Decode-first objective; TTFT becomes non-gating; workload cost-role staging

#### Added

- C-WORKLOAD-STAGING — short-output workloads drive the search; long-output workloads reserved for final-report characterization

#### Changed

- C-OBJECTIVE — decode latency/throughput is the first-class objective and the sole SLO gate; TTFT is now a reported, non-gating metric
- C-OBJECTIVE — throughput axis is steady-state decode throughput tagged with context length, not OSL-averaged

### v0.1.0 (2026-06-09)

Initial draft
